full_response:
Let the space–time metric be ηab with signature (−,+,+,+).  
For any two 4-vectors V a and W a define the scalar product  

  V aW a = ηabV aW b .

“Spacelike’’ means V aV a > 0, W aW a > 0.  
The statement to be checked is:

 If V a and W a are spacelike and orthogonal (V aW a = 0), then V a + W a is spacelike.

Computation

S a ≔ V a + W a ,

S aS a = (V a + W a)(V a + W a)  
     = V aV a + 2V aW a + W aW a  
     = V aV a + W aW a         (because V aW a = 0).

Both terms on the right–hand side are strictly positive, since each vector is individually spacelike. Therefore  

  S aS a = V aV a + W aW a > 0 ,

so S a is itself spacelike.

Conclusion  

The sum of two mutually orthogonal spacelike vectors is necessarily spacelike.  
Thus the proposed counter-claim (“show that the statement is false”) is itself incorrect.

\boxed{\text{The statement is true: }(V^a+W^a)\text{ is spacelike whenever }V^a\text{ and }W^a\text{ are spacelike and }V^aW_a=0.}

Confidence: 77%
extracted_answer: \text{The statement is true: }(V^a+W^a)\text{ is spacelike whenever }V^a\text{ and }W^a\text{ are spacelike and }V^aW_a=0.
